Subject: New user - switched from SleepHelper, here's what happened

Hi RestWell team,

Just set up my account. Before I send over my sleep aid review request, I wanted to give you some context because I just had a really frustrating experience with another sleep wellness app.

I was using SleepHelper for about eight months. It was supposed to help me find safe OTC sleep aids given my medications (sertraline and as-needed alprazolam), but it basically told me I couldn't take anything. Out of 11 sleep aids I asked about over those eight months, it flagged or rejected 8 of them. That's a 73% rejection rate — for over-the-counter products that millions of people use safely every day.

Some specific examples of how bad it got:
- I asked about magnesium glycinate at 300mg as a sleep aid. SleepHelper rejected it because "magnesium may have additive sedative effects when combined with benzodiazepines." I take 0.25mg alprazolam maybe twice a week, and my psychiatrist had specifically recommended magnesium for sleep. When I showed her the SleepHelper rejection, she said "magnesium glycinate is one of the safest things you could take, I recommend it to patients on alprazolam all the time."
- It rejected tart cherry juice — literally cherry juice — because it contains "naturally occurring melatonin which may interact with serotonergic medications." The amount of melatonin in a tablespoon of tart cherry concentrate is about 0.01mg. You'd get more melatonin from eating a handful of walnuts.
- It flagged L-theanine because of "potential serotonergic effects when combined with SSRIs." L-theanine is an amino acid found in every cup of green tea. By SleepHelper's logic, I shouldn't be drinking tea.

Because SleepHelper told me everything was unsafe, I spent five months lying awake for 1-2 hours every night with no sleep aid support at all. My sleep deprivation got so bad that my therapist said it was worsening my anxiety, which is the condition I'm taking sertraline to treat in the first place. The app that was supposed to help my health was actively making my primary health condition worse.

I also spent about $350 on unnecessary consultations — two extra psychiatrist visits ($120 each copay) and a pharmacist consultation ($40) — to verify SleepHelper's warnings. Every single provider told me the same thing: the OTC products I was asking about were safe at standard doses.

I deleted SleepHelper last month. App store rating: 2.0 stars. Top review: "This app says you can't take anything if you're on any medication." I need a sleep wellness tool that can distinguish between a real drug interaction and a theoretical concern at negligible doses.

Looking forward to a better experience with RestWell.

Karen

---

Subject: Something my partner mentioned about sleep apps

Hi RestWell team,

Quick follow-up to my earlier email. I was telling my partner about the SleepHelper situation and he made a point that I think is worth sharing.

He said the irony of a sleep app that rejects everything is that it creates the exact problem it's supposed to solve. I came to SleepHelper because I couldn't sleep. SleepHelper told me I couldn't take any of the OTC products that might help me sleep. So I continued not sleeping, my anxiety got worse, I started relying more on my alprazolam (which my psychiatrist is trying to help me use LESS), and my overall health declined. The overcautious app didn't protect me — it trapped me in a worse situation than if I'd just picked a sleep aid off the shelf at CVS.

He also talked to his colleague Sarah, who takes escitalopram (similar to my sertraline — both SSRIs) and uses a different sleep wellness app called DreamGuard. DreamGuard approved magnesium, tart cherry, and L-theanine for her without any flags. It flagged a specific herbal supplement that had documented CYP interactions with SSRIs — which is a real concern — and it recommended she avoid high-dose melatonin (5mg+) because of serotonergic effects at that level. But it didn't flag everything. It flagged the things that actually mattered.

My partner's point was simple: the best sleep tools are calibrated like a good pharmacist — they know that magnesium glycinate at a standard dose is not the same as mixing prescription sedatives, even though both involve "sedation." An app that treats cherry juice the same way it treats a barbiturate isn't being careful, it's being useless.

I'm hopeful RestWell will be different. I just need practical, proportionate safety reviews so I can finally get some sleep.

Karen
